elements.sent-2411-001: In this short note, we provide a new infinite family of $K_{2, t+1}$-free graphs for each prime power $t$.
elements.sent-2411-002: Using these graphs, we show that it is possible to partition the edges of $K_n$ into parts, such that each part is isomorphic to our $K_{2, t+1}$-free graph.
elements.sent-2411-003: This yields an improved lower bound to the multicolor Ramsey number $r_k(K_{2, t+1 )$ when $k$ and $t$ are powers of the same prime.}
elements.sent-2411-004: For these values of $k$ and $t$, our coloring implies that \begin{equation}tk^2 + 1 \leq r_k(K_{2, t+1}) \leq tk^2 + k + 2.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-006: The Ramsey number $r_k(F)$ denotes the smallest number of vertices $n$ such that in any $k$-coloring of the complete graph $K_n$, there exists a monochromatic copy of $F$ (i.e. $F$ is a subgraph of one of the color classes).
elements.sent-2411-007: Ramsey theory has received much attention over the last century with a number of ground breaking results appearing the last few years \cite{AFM, LW, CSMS, MaV, Y .}
elements.sent-2411-008: We contribute to this area by improving the lower bound for $r_k(K_{2, t+1 )$ for infinitely many $k$ and $t$.}
elements.sent-2411-009: Chung and Graham \cite{CG proved that \begin{equation}r_k(K_{2, {t+1}}) \leq \left\{ {cc} k^2 + k + 1 if t=1 tk^2 + k + 2 if t > 1  \right.\end{equation}.}}
elements.formula-2411-002: \begin{equation}r_k(K_{2, {t+1}}) \leq \left\{ {cc} k^2 + k + 1 if t=1 tk^2 + k + 2 if t > 1  \right.}\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-010: They conjectured that when $t >> S$, $r_k(K_{s, t ) = (t-1)k^s + o(k^s)$.}
elements.sent-2411-011: Chung \cite{C2 in her dissertation had demonstrated that when $s = 2$, \begin{equation}\lim_{t \rightarrow \infty } \frac{r_k(K_{2, t})}{t} = k^2\end{equation} implying the correct asymptotics up the constant term of $t$ in the conjecture.}
elements.sent-2411-012: However, it was not until Axenovich, F\"{u redi, and Mubayi \cite{AFM that the conjecture was verified for $s = 2$.}}
elements.sent-2411-013: Their coloring relied on the construction of F\"{u redi of a dense $K_{2, t}$-free graphs \cite{ZF .}}
elements.sent-2411-014: In particular, the result from \cite{AFM implies that given any integer $t$, and there are infinitely many $k$ for which a graph of order $tk^2$ can be colored with $k + O(\sqrt{tk}\log (tk))$ colors such that each color class is $K_{2, t+1}$-free.}
elements.sent-2411-015: For large $k$, this roughly implies the lower bound \begin{equation}\label{eqAFM} tk^2 - c_tk^{3/2}\log(k) \leq r_k(K_{2, t+1})\end{equation} for some constant $c_t$ dependent on $t$.
elements.sent-2411-016: The authors leverage a prime density argument to demonstrate that the leading term asymptotics are correct.
elements.sent-2411-017: In this paper, we give an explicit coloring which removes the lower order term in (\ref{eqAFM ) entirely when $k$ and $t$ are any powers of the same prime.}
elements.sent-2411-018: For $s = 2$ and $t = 1$, incremental progress has been made by Chung \cite{C , Irving \cite{I , and most recently by Lazebnik and Woldar \cite{LW .}}}
elements.sent-2411-019: In \cite{LW , the authors gave an explicit coloring which showed that $r_k(K_{2, 2 ) \geq k^2 + 2$ when $k$ is an odd prime power.}}
elements.sent-2411-020: Thus, when $k$ is an odd prime power we have the bounds \begin{equation}\label{lazeq} k^2 + 2 \leq r_k(K_{2, 2}) \leq k^2 + k + 1.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-021: We will demonstrate that lower bound extends to the case when $k$ is an even prime power as well.
elements.sent-2411-022: Let $ex(n, F)$ denote the maximum number of edges in an $F$-free graph on $n$ vertices.
elements.sent-2411-023: This function is called the Tur\'{a n number of $F$.}
elements.sent-2411-024: The Ramsey number $r_k(F)$ and Tur\'{a n number are related in the following way: If $r_k(F) = n + 1$, then \begin{equation}ex(n, F) \geq \frac{1}{k}\binom{n}{2}.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-025: F\"{u redi's construction of $K_{2, t+1}$-free graphs gives the best-known lower bound for $ex(n, K_{2, t+1 )$ \cite{ZF .}}}
elements.sent-2411-026: Together with the best-known upper bound due to Hylt\'{e n-Cavallius \cite{HC (which is a small improvement over the famous K\"{o v\'{a ri, S\'{o s, and Tur\'{a n theorem \cite{KST ), we have that \begin{equation}\frac{\sqrt{t}}{2}n^{3/2} - \frac{n}{2} < ex(n, K_{2, t+1}) < \frac{1}{2}n\sqrt{tn - t + 1/4} + \frac{n}{4}\end{equation} for infinitely many $n$.}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-027: The lower order term in the lower bound can be improved for specific $n$ by performing a case by case analysis of F\"{u redi's construction.}
elements.sent-2411-028: A prime density argument implies that the leading term asymptotics in this construction are correct for all $n$.
elements.sent-2411-029: Until now, F\"{u redi's construction is the only know construction capable of producing infinite families of $K_{2, t+1}$-free graphs with the right edge count and for infinitely many $t$.}
elements.sent-2411-030: In this paper, we generalize the approach of Lazebnik and Woldar \cite{LW to give two main results.}
elements.sent-2411-031: The first is a new construction of $K_{2, t+1}$-free graphs which is yields an equal or slightly improved lower bound for $ex(n, K_{2, t+1 )$ over F\"{u redi's construction when $t$ is a prime power.}}
elements.sent-2411-032: If $n = q^2/t$, then \begin{equation}\frac{\sqrt{t}}{2}n^{3/2} - \frac{\sqrt{tn}}{2} \leq ex(n, K_{2, t+1}).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-033: Just like in \cite{LW , a nice property of our coloring is all the color classes are all isomorphic to our construction which yields Theorem \ref{thm1 .}}
elements.sent-2411-034: Such decompositions of $K_n$ are exceedingly rare, and algebraically defined graphs seem to be the only known class of graphs which seem to be able to consistently produce such decompositions \cite{LW1 .}
elements.sent-2411-035: Given a positive integer $d < e$, let $f(x)$ be an $\F_p$-linear polynomial (i.e. $f(x + y) = f(x) + f(y)$) in $\F_q[x]$ with $p^d$ roots.
elements.sent-2411-036: We note that such a polynomial always exists as it can be thought of as a linear transformation of rank $e - d$ on the vector space $\F_p^e$.
elements.sent-2411-037: Denote the range of $f(x)$ by the set $ R_f = \{ f(x) : x \in \F_q \}.
elements.sent-2411-038: Define the graph $\Gamma_{f}$ with vertex set $V = \F_{q}\times R_{f}$.
elements.sent-2411-039: Two distinct vertices $(v_1, v_2), (w_1, w_2) \in V$ are adjacent if and only if \begin{equation}\label{adjeq} v_2 + w_2 = f(v_1w_1).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-040: Let $t = p^d$ and note that $|V| = q|R_f| = q^2/t$.
elements.sent-2411-042: Observe that given any vertex $v = (v_1, v_2)$, and any $\alpha \in \F_{q}$, $\alpha \neq v_1$, the vertex $(v_1, v_2)$ has a unique neighbor with first coordinate $\alpha$.
elements.sent-2411-043: In particular, using (\ref{adjeq ), we have that $v$ is adjacent to $(\alpha, f(\alpha v_1) - v_2)$.}
elements.sent-2411-044: Let $v = (v_1, v_2)$ and $w = (w_1, w_2)$ be two vertices in $\Gamma$, we will show that they cannot have more than $t$ common neighbors.
elements.sent-2411-045: If $v_1 = w_1$, then $v$ and $w$ share no common neighbors because if they did, it would imply that this common neighbor is adjacent to two distinct vertices with same first coordinate, a contradiction.
elements.sent-2411-047: Let $x = (x_1, x_2) \in V$ be a common neighbor of both $v$ and $w$.
elements.sent-2411-048: Then the equations \begin{equation}x_2 + v_2 = f(x_1v_1) x_2 + w_2 = f(x_1w_1)\end{equation} must be satisfied by $(x_1, x_2)$.
elements.sent-2411-049: Subtracting equation (5) from equation (4), we obtain that \begin{equation}\label{eq2} v_2 - w_2 = f(x_1(v_1 - w_1)).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-050: Thus if $x$ is adjacent to $v$ and $w$, it's first coordinate must be a solution to (\ref{eq2 ).}
elements.sent-2411-051: Since $v_1 \neq w_1$, then we know that the polynomial on the right hand side is not 0.
elements.sent-2411-052: Let $a = v_1 - w_1$ and $b = v_2 - w_2$, then $x_1$ satisfies \begin{equation}f(a x_1) = b\end{equation} Since $b \in R_f$, then we know that there exists at least one solution.
elements.sent-2411-053: On the other hand, $f(x)$ is $\F_p$-linear over $\F_{q}$ and has $t = p^d$ roots and so must in-fact be a $t$-to-$1$ mapping.
elements.sent-2411-054: Therefore, there are exactly $t$ different values of $x_1$ which satisfy (\ref{eq2 ), and so $v$ and $w$ have at most $t$ common neighbors.}
elements.sent-2411-056: To determine the exact number of edges in $\Gamma_{f}$ we need to count the number of vertices of degree $q$ and degree $q-1$.
elements.sent-2411-057: Note that the beginning of the proof of the claim above implies that each vertex has degree at least $q - 1$.
elements.sent-2411-058: To account for the remaining edges, note that adjacency relation (\ref{adjeq ) implies that some vertices $(v_1, v_2)$ are adjacent to distinct vertices with same first coordinate, $(v_1, v_3)$.}
elements.sent-2411-059: In particular, we have $(v_1, v_2)$ is adjacent to $(v_1, v_3)$ if and only if \begin{equation}v_2 + v_3 = f(v_1^2).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-061: A vertex has degree $(q-1)$ if the equation above only holds when $v_2 = v_3$.
elements.sent-2411-062: So we count the number of solutions in $V$ to the equation. \begin{equation}2v_2 = f(v_1^2)\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-063: If $q$ is even, then $(v_1, v_2)$ is a solution of $0 = f(v_1^2)$ if and only if $v_1^2$ is a root of $f(x)$.
elements.sent-2411-064: Since $x^2$ is a permutation of $\F_q$ then we know that there are $t$ such values of $v_1$.
elements.sent-2411-065: The second coordinate $v_2$ belongs to $R_f$, so there are $t|R_f| = q$ solutions.
elements.sent-2411-066: If $q$ is odd, then we have $2v_2 = f(v_1^2)$.
elements.sent-2411-067: For each $v_1 \in \F_q$, there exists a unique $v_2$ which solves the equation.
elements.sent-2411-068: Thus there are $q$ solutions to this equation as well.
elements.sent-2411-069: Consequently, $\Gamma$ has $q$ vertices of degree $(q-1)$ and $(q^2/t) - q$ vertices of degree $q$.
elements.sent-2411-070: Let $n = |V| = q^2/t$, then the number of edges in $\Gamma_{f}$ is \begin{equation}\frac{1}{2}\left(q\left(\frac{q^2}{t} - q\right) + (q - 1)q \right) = \frac{q^3}{2t} - \frac{q}{2} = \frac{\sqrt{t}}{2}n^{3/2} - \frac{\sqrt{tn}}{2}\end{equation} This finishes the proof of Theorem \ref{thm1 .}
elements.sent-2411-071: We now demonstrate how the graphs $\Gamma_f$ can be used to partition the edges of $K_n$, when $n = q^2/t$, into $k = |R_f|$ color classes, such that each class is $K_{2, t+1}$-free.
elements.sent-2411-072: Denote the $k$ colors by the elements of $R_f$ and label the vertices of $K_n$ by the set $V = \F_q \times R_f$.
elements.sent-2411-073: Assign the color $\alpha \in R_f$ to the edge between $(v_1, v_2)$ and $(w_1, w_2)$ if \begin{equation}v_2 + w_2 + \alpha = f(v_1w_1).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-074: Clearly, no edge can be assigned two different colors in this way.
elements.sent-2411-075: Furthermore, if $(v_1, v_2)$ and $(w_1, w_2)$ are distinct vertices, then setting \begin{equation}\alpha = f(v_1w_1) - v_2 - w_2,\end{equation} demonstrates that this edge belongs to the color class $\alpha$ and so each edge receives a color.
elements.sent-2411-076: Denote by $\Gamma_{f, \alpha}$ the subgraph of $K_n$ defined by the color class $\alpha$.
elements.sent-2411-078: Here we will provide an isomorphism depending on if $q$ is odd or $q$ is even.
elements.sent-2411-079: It is straight forward to verify the isomorphism.
elements.sent-2411-080: If $q$ is odd, define $\Phi_\alpha: V \rightarrow V$ by \begin{equation}\Phi_\alpha((v_1, v_2)) = (v_1, v_2 - 2^{-1}\alpha)\end{equation} Then $\Phi_\alpha$ is an isomorphism from $\Gamma_{f}$ to $\Gamma_{f, \alpha}$.
elements.sent-2411-081: In particular, we observe that $\Phi_\alpha((v_1, v_2))$ is adjacent to $\Phi_\alpha((w_1, w_2))$ in $\Gamma_{f, \alpha}$ if and only if \begin{equation}(v_2 - 2^{-1}\alpha) + (w_2 - 2^{-1}\alpha) + \alpha = f(v_1w_1) \iff v_2 + w_2 = f(v_1w_1).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-082: The last equality holds true if and only if $(v_1, v_2)$ is adjacent to $(w_1, w_2)$ in $\Gamma_f$.
elements.sent-2411-083: If $q$ is even, then $x^2$ is a permutation of $\F_q$.
elements.sent-2411-084: Thus, for each $\alpha \in R_{f}$, there exists a solution to $f(x^2) = \alpha$.
elements.sent-2411-085: Let $\beta$ be such a solution and define $\Phi_{\beta : V \rightarrow V$ by \begin{equation}\Phi_\beta((v_1, v_2)) = (v_1 + \beta, v_2 + f(\beta v_1)).\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-086: Then $\Phi_\beta$ is an isomorphism from $\Gamma_{f}$ to $\Gamma_{f, \alpha}$.
elements.sent-2411-087: A similar calculation as in the odd case verifies the isomorphism.
elements.sent-2411-088: Consequently, each of the $k$ color classes is $K_{2,t +1}$-free.
elements.sent-2411-089: Note that the only constraint on the relationship between $k$ and $t$ is that $q = kt$.
elements.sent-2411-090: Thus, given any two powers of the same prime $k$ and $t$, and setting $q = kt$, the above implies Theorem \ref{thm2 .}

elements.sent-2411-118: The method used here for constructing $K_{2, t+1}$-free graphs can likely be extended to $K_{3, t}$-free graphs if only we had an algebraically defined $K_{3, 3}$-free graph to work with as a base.
elements.sent-2411-119: To date, we have only one infinite family of $K_{3, 3}$-free graphs with asymptotically the right number of edges which was constructed by Brown \cite{Brown .}
elements.sent-2411-120: It is natural to ask if a new $K_{3, 3}$-free family can be constructed from algebraically defined graphs.
elements.sent-2411-121: Problem: Find an infinite family algebraically defined $K_{3, 3}$-free graphs of order $n$ with approximately $n^{1 + 2/3}$ edges.
